textStyles.instructions: Primary on-screen title: short, high impact, often a single claim or section title. Prefer one line; two only if both are short.
Supporting line: subtext, qualification, or CTA. Lighter weight than the title; use for subheads, caveats, and “what to do next”.

characters.name: Borrower
characters.description: Main viewer stand-in: a self-employed person trying to get a mortgage. Smart-casual, approachable, not a caricature. Show a clear arc from stressed or overwhelmed (paperwork, uncertainty) to calmer and more confident once things make sense. Use for empathy shots, POV moments, and reactions at a kitchen table or desk.

characters.name: Lender
characters.description: Represents a bank or building society: professional, neutral, trustworthy. Explains rules in plain language—affordability, deposits, documents—not salesy. Typical props: branded folder, desk, screen with forms. Good for “what the bank needs from you” beats.

characters.name: Accountant
characters.description: Calm, numbers-first helper: reconciling books, SA302s, tax years, and declared income. Setting can be a small practice, home office, or meeting with the borrower. Laptop, printouts, calculator energy. Use when the story is about proving income from accounts, not just payslips.

characters.name: Broker
characters.description: Whole-of-market mortgage adviser: consultative, compares options, sits between borrower and lenders. Often with a laptop, comparison notes, or a phone call in frame. Use for “shopping the market,” explaining product types, or walking through an application step-by-step.

characters.name: Employee
characters.description: A salaried PAYE worker for contrast: payslips, P60, simpler proof of income. Same friendly tone as the borrower but fewer moving parts—use in split-screen or before/after comparisons when you need to show how self-employed underwriting differs from standard employment.
